ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Chicken Tikka. Nearest parent is a marinated grilled chicken product. A new concept for Indian tandoor-cooked chicken preparations is recommended.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of chicken tikka is 6 pieces (approximately 200g total) of boneless marinated chicken with charred edges from the tandoor, served with mint chutney, sliced onion, and a wedge of lemon.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Tikka — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, Entry F11020024; cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Exact match in ICMR-NIN under entry F11020024 — Chicken Tikka, boneless marinated grilled preparation.

gi_source: GI testing not applicable — Chicken Tikka contains negligible carbohydrates (3.5g/100g); it cannot meaningfully raise blood glucose.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium approximately 850mg per serving (200g) — elevated; the marinade and dry-rub spice blend contain significant salt; restaurant versions are often high sodium.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit to half a serving (100g) or request reduced-salt preparation. The chicken itself is an excellent source of potassium and protein for cardiovascular health.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Virtually no carbohydrates; GI testing not applicable. High protein (22.5g/100g) and moderate fat support satiety and blood glucose stability.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Excellent choice for diabetic patients — chicken tikka provides high protein with negligible glycaemic impact. Pair with salad or raita instead of naan or paratha to maintain the low-carbohydrate profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Excellent source of selenium (28mcg/100g) which is a key antioxidant for immune function; turmeric in the spice blend provides curcumin; very high niacin and B6.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Chicken tikka is one of the better lean protein options for patients with rheumatoid arthritis. The spice blend provides anti-inflammatory compounds. Selenium supports glutathione peroxidase — a key antioxidant enzyme.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Tikka is one of the most beloved Indian starter dishes — boneless pieces of chicken (breast or thigh) marinated in a yogurt and spice marinade (ginger-garlic paste, red chilli, cumin, coriander, garam masala, turmeric, and lemon) for several hours, then skewered and cooked at high heat in a tandoor clay oven until charred at the edges and cooked through. Served without sauce as a dry starter, garnished with sliced onion, lemon wedges, and mint chutney. Very high in protein, low in carbohydrates, and rich in selenium and B vitamins.
common_user_queries: chicken tikka vs chicken tikka masala

confidence_reason: Exact match found in ICMR-NIN under entry F11020024 — Chicken Tikka, boneless grilled preparation.
notes: Contains dairy (yogurt in marinade at trace levels after cooking). Not suitable for vegans or strict dairy-free diets. Cholesterol approximately 156mg per serving (200g). GI and GL are null — carbohydrate content is negligible. Grilling over direct flame may create heterocyclic amines (HCAs) — avoid charred portions to minimise carcinogen exposure.
